setting.note: On the island of Penang, the half-English, half-Chinese Philip Hutton is taken as a pupil by the Japanese diplomat Endo-san, who teaches him aiki-jutsu and a hard code of duty, only for the bond to be tested to breaking when Japan invades Malaya in 1941. Tan maps his home island: the Hutton family's George Town mansion and the colonial society of the port, the small island off the coast where Endo-san lives and teaches, the town under brutal occupation, and Penang Hill above it all.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. Tan Twan Eng, first published 2007. Mapped as a cited work: no text stored, no passages reproduced. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. No reproduction of the copyrighted text, in any territory.
rights.attribution: The Gift of Rain, Tan Twan Eng (2007). Locations mapped by Read the Places; not affiliated with or endorsed by the author or publisher.

palette.note: The blue-grey of the Straits and of the monsoon rain that gives the book its title, the water around the island where duty and betrayal meet. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
